title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) Vaccine Safety | Vaccine Safety | CDC
text:

Pfizer's RSV vaccine ( Abrysvo ) was approved in 2023. Pfizer RSV vaccine is approved for adults ages 60 years and adults ages 18 to 59 years who are at increased risk of RSV disease to prevent LRTD caused by RSV. 
GSK's RSV vaccine ( Arexvy ) was approved in 2023. GSK RSV vaccine is approved for adults ages 60 years and older and people ages 50 to 59 years who are at increased risk of RSV disease to prevent LRTD caused by RSV. 
Moderna's RSV vaccine ( mResvia ) was originally approved in 2024 for adults ages 60 years and older to prevent LRTD caused by RSV. In 2025, Moderna was also licensed for adults ages 18-59 who are at increased risk for severe RSV disease. 
For infants and certain young children 
Fact 
CDC recommends one of two immunization options to protect all babies from severe RSV: A maternal RSV vaccine given to the mother during pregnancy or an RSV antibody given to your baby. Most babies do not need both to protect against severe RSV. 
FDA approved nirsevimab (Beyfortus) in 2023. Nirsevimab is an injectable monoclonal antibody for the prevention of LRTD caused by RSV. 
Nirsevimab is approved for infants born during or entering their first RSV season and for some young children who are at increased risk for severe RSV disease and entering their second RSV season. 
For pregnant women (maternal RSV vaccine)
